data.title: Southern upgraded at KeyBanc after shares have lagged utility peers
data.description:
Southern Co. (SO) +1.2% in Wednesday's trading as KeyBanc upgraded to Sector Weight from Underweight, saying the shares have largely lost their historical premium after underperforming utility peers over the past 12 months.

Southern (SO) now trades in line with its large-cap, vertically integrated peer average of 19.2x, and KeyBanc's Sophie Karp believes the stock is fairly valued, and recent earnings growth outlook revision and constructive news flow should further support the shares.

The company recently raised its long-term earnings per share compound annual growth rate to as high as 8% through 2030 on the back of accelerating large load additions and resulting 10% sales growth, and rate base growth is now forecast at 9%, which indicates a fairly modest 100 bps regulatory lag, Karp said.

Southern (SO) is aiming to achieve this growth during the rate stay-out in its key Georgia and Alabama territories through 2029, which should reduce any possible regulatory overhangs elsewhere, the analyst said, adding the new growth targets bring the company more in line with its higher-growing, large-cap, vertically integrated peers.

data.title: Evolus posts record rally after Q4 beat
data.description:
Evolus (EOLS) shares jumped ~36% on Wednesday, its biggest intraday gain in nearly half a decade, after the maker of Jeuveau anti-wrinkle therapy exceeded Street forecasts with its Q4 2025 financials, posting net income for the first time in nearly five years.

On Tuesday after the close, the California-based beauty company reported $90.3M in revenue for the quarter, beating the consensus by $0.7M with ~14% YoY growth thanks to higher volumes of Jeuveau and other products.

Evolus (EOLS) also posted quarterly net income for the first time since Q1 2021 after its net income for Q4 reached $130K, compared to a net loss of $6.7M in the prior year period. However, its gross margin slipped to 65.7% from 66.7% in the year-ago period, while GAAP EPS of $0.00 exceeded Street estimates by $0.05.

The company projected $327M - $337M in net revenue for this year, indicating 10% YoY - 13% YoY growth from 2025, and estimated $450M - $500M in total net revenue for 2028 with a three-year CAGR of 15% - 19%.

However, its projection of 65.5%–67.0% for adjusted gross profit margin in 2026 suggested a decline from 67.4% and 69.6% recorded in 2025 and 2024, respectively.

data.title: CoStar Group insiders, including CEO, buy shares after stock slides
data.description:
CoStar Group's (CSGP) CEO, president of Marketplaces, and one of its directors disclosed this week purchases of stock totaling 76,720 shares as the company's stock price declined by 28% year-to-date.

President and CEO Andrew Florance purchased 55,720 shares at an average price of $44.52 per share, adding up to a $2.48M purchase, according to a filing on Tuesday. That brings his total holdings in the company to just under 1.59M shares.

Frederick Saint, president of the company's Marketplaces unit, acquired 20,000 shares at an average of $45.33 per share on March 2, a $906.6K outlay. However, the executive also sold 11,096 shares at $44.63 per share, or ~$495K, on March 1. After both transactions, Saint holds 279,851 shares of CoStar (CSGP).

Director Rachel Glaser paid $44.94 per share for 1,000 shares, a $44,940 purchase on March 2. Her total holdings come to 4,157 shares.

CoStar (CSGP) stock rose 2.9% in Wednesday afternoon trading.

data.title: Fortitude Gold outlines $40M joint venture and rapid mine expansion as permitting accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Fortitude Gold Corporation (FTCO) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Jason Reid highlighted a year of overcoming significant permitting challenges, stating, "2025 was a year of overcoming challenges, including the hangover from the long and arduous permit backlogs caused by the Biden administration and its anti-mining BLM." He underscored that new permits for County Line and Scarlet South were secured under the Trump administration, which he described as a turning point for the company.
  • The company reported $18.4 million in net sales, a $4.7 million cash balance at year-end, 5,236 ounces of gold and 32,809 ounces of silver produced, $29.5 million in working capital, $5.8 million in dividends paid, $0.4 million net income, $10 million mine gross profit, and $6.3 million in exploration expenditures.
  • In response to prolonged federal permitting delays, Fortitude Gold reduced its dividend by 75%, suspended all exploration drill programs, cut its exploration team, relocated to a smaller office, and eliminated company-wide bonuses.
  • The company transitioned to an owner-operator mining model, bringing mining operations in-house and projecting "a significant reduction in per ton mining costs and improved operational control." (CEO Jason Reid)
  • To support mine development and exploration, Fortitude completed a private placement, selling 2.5 million shares at $4.75 per share for $12 million in proceeds. Reid said, "Sometimes dilution is warranted. This was one of those times."
  • The company announced a $40 million joint venture with Hawthorne Land & Minerals, LLC for the East Camp Douglas property, retaining 60% ownership and operator status. Reid described this as "a transformational moment for the property to advance it on an expedited basis."

Outlook

  • Management indicated a renewed ability to pursue aggressive exploration and mine expansion, targeting additional mine permits under the current administration. Reid explained, "Our goal is to obtain as many additional permits as possible under the Trump administration in case the American people vote back in a Biden-Harris anti-business anti-mining administration in the future."
  • The company expects to deploy $40 million at East Camp Douglas over the next 2 years, aiming for rapid discovery and potential new mine development.
  • No production guidance was provided for 2026, with Reid stating, "We might not even forecast this year. We have so many balls in the air. We're bringing on 2 new mines, a lot of moving parts. Whatever we would forecast would likely be wrong. So I think we're just best not to."

Financial Results

  • Fortitude Gold reported $18.4 million in net sales, $4.7 million in cash at year-end, $29.5 million in working capital, $5.8 million in dividends paid, $0.4 million net income, $10 million mine gross profit, $6.3 million in exploration expenditures, $1,104 total cash after by-product credits per gold ounce sold, and $1,697 per ounce total all-in sustaining cost.
  • The quarter saw 5,236 gold ounces and 32,809 silver ounces produced.
  • Post-capital raise, the company has 26.8 million shares outstanding.

Q&A

  • Peter G asked about the recent capital raise and its impact on dilution. Reid responded that the raise was necessary and highlighted the company's "incredibly tight capital structure."
  • Dr. Joe C inquired about processing capacity, mine sequencing, and production stabilization. Reid explained that a larger 800 tonne-per-hour crusher had been installed and that heap leach expansion is in the works, but declined to provide specific production forecasts, emphasizing the focus on maximizing mine life.
  • Gary [indiscernible] asked about the joint venture valuation and cash generation. Reid described East Camp Douglas' geological potential and clarified that JV terms allow Fortitude to retain control, with Hawthorne funding up to $40 million before Fortitude contributes further capital.
  • Multiple questions addressed the use of JV funds, return on investment for Pearl Deep, drill crew availability, hiring challenges, equipment leasing, sulfide gold strategy, and dividend policy. Reid consistently stressed the focus on long-term production and exploration, and the company's commitment to rebuilding dividend capacity over time.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' questions reflected cautious optimism, with a focus on the rationale behind the capital raise, the scale and speed of exploration, and the company's ability to deliver value from new projects amid challenging industry conditions.
  • Management's tone was assertive and occasionally defensive when discussing the necessity of dilution and the company's strategy following regulatory delays. Reid frequently referenced overcoming adversity, the strategic value of recent actions, and the positive impact of new permitting processes.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management showed increased optimism, with management expressing more confidence in the company's future given the improved permitting environment and new joint venture partnership.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language shifted from caution and operational restraint in Q3 2025 to a more expansive, forward-looking outlook in Q4, enabled by new permits and JV funding.
  • Strategic focus moved from cost-cutting and survival to aggressive exploration, mine development, and capital deployment.
  • Key metrics improved: net sales rose from $4.7 million in Q3 to $18.4 million in Q4, gold production increased from 1,384 ounces to 5,236 ounces, and exploration expenditures quadrupled.
  • Analysts' questions in Q4 centered on growth opportunities, project execution, and capital allocation, whereas Q3 focused more on operational constraints and permitting progress.
  • Management's tone was significantly more confident in Q4, citing "a very exciting path forward" and describing 2026 prospects as "incredible."

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified ongoing risks related to future political changes that could impact mine permitting, emphasizing the need to secure as many permits as possible under the current administration.
  • The company faces challenges in hiring skilled exploration and drilling teams due to industry-wide labor shortages.
  • Questions about the return on investment for the Pearl Deep project highlighted uncertainty in resource quantification and production outcomes.
  • Reid acknowledged the complexity of forecasting production due to multiple new mines coming online and variable heap leach residuals, opting not to provide formal guidance.

Final Takeaway

Fortitude Gold Corporation ended 2025 having weathered a period of intense regulatory and operational challenges, but with the benefit of new permits, a transformational $40 million joint venture, and a strategic capital raise to fund exploration and mine development. Management signaled a shift from survival mode to aggressive expansion, with a focus on maximizing mine life, accelerating discovery at East Camp Douglas, and positioning the company for long-term shareholder value as industry conditions become more favorable.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: National Vision outlines $2.03B–$2.09B 2026 revenue target while expanding premium offerings and store segmentation
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: National Vision Holdings, Inc. (EYE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO Alex Wilkes described 2025 as a year of transformation for National Vision, highlighting the enhancement of the leadership team and the launch of a modernization roadmap aimed at "modernizing every aspect of the business, from customer engagement and in-store digital tools, to product, pricing, presentation, data and technology infrastructure." Wilkes stated the company achieved "strong mid-single-digit comp store sales and modernized our business, all while driving significant operating margin expansion."
  • Wilkes emphasized the shift toward a more profitable customer mix, stating, "We're expanding our customer base with more profitable customers such as those who use managed care or progressive lenses or bringing a prescription from another doctor or what we call outside Rx."
  • Product initiatives drove results, with new branded frames and premium assortments introduced, and Wilkes noted, "We ended the year with approximately 40% of frames for sale in our stores priced above $99, up from 20% at the start of the year."
  • The company also highlighted the rollout of Meta AI glasses, now available in 300 stores with expectations for full deployment by the end of Q2 2026. Wilkes commented, "Since launching Meta AI glasses, last spring, our sell-through has exceeded expectations."
  • Christopher Laden, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "The culmination of these efforts resulted in a 160 basis point expansion in operating margin in 2025. This progress reflects the dedication and alignment of our entire team, and I'm confident in our ability to build on these achievements as we enter fiscal 2026."

Outlook

  • For fiscal 2026, National Vision expects net revenue between $2.03 billion and $2.09 billion, supported by adjusted comparable store sales growth of 3% to 6%.
  • Adjusted operating income is projected between $107 million and $133 million, with adjusted operating margin expansion of approximately 100 basis points relative to fiscal 2025, excluding the 53rd week.
  • Adjusted diluted EPS is expected to range from $0.85 to $1.09 per share.
  • The company plans capital expenditures between $73 million and $78 million, with investments to open approximately 30 to 35 new stores and close 10 to 15 stores as part of ongoing fleet optimization.
  • Store openings will be weighted towards America's Best brand stores, and closures will begin in Q2 and continue through Q4.

Financial Results

  • Fiscal 2025 net revenue grew 9% to $1.99 billion.
  • Adjusted comp store sales were 6%, and adjusted operating income rose 56% to $102.5 million versus $65.5 million in 2024, with AOI margin at 5.2%.
  • Adjusted EPS for fiscal 2025 was $0.80 per share compared with $0.52 in 2024.
  • Q4 net revenue increased 15.1% to $503 million, adjusted comp store sales were 4.8%, and adjusted operating income was $17.6 million with AOI margin improving to 3.5% from 0.7% in Q4 last year.
  • Adjusted EPS in Q4 was $0.15 per share compared to a loss of $0.04 per share last year.
  • The 53rd week contributed $35.6 million in net revenue and $3.5 million in adjusted operating income for the year.

Q&A

  • Michael Lasser, UBS: Questioned the sustainability of the shift toward managed care and premium customers and its effect on growth. Wilkes responded, "We are still being a bit more conservative on our traffic expectations with the cash pay consumer until we start to see a bit more acceleration in the repurchase cycle of that consumer cohort."
  • Simeon Siegel, Guggenheim: Asked about the duration of the customer mix shift. Wilkes replied, "We think we have multiple years of work ahead of us to be very deliberate on our consumer cohort shift."
  • Dylan Carden, William Blair: Inquired about industry softness and CapEx. Wilkes indicated, "We control our own destiny on a lot of these elements," and Laden added, "We can refresh the stores, we can introduce in the new branding assets at a pretty nominal rate for CapEx relative to the size of the business."
  • Brian Tanquilut, Jefferies: Asked about the stickiness of the middle-income customer. Wilkes said, "We feel even better about it today than we would have a year ago because that middle-income consumer has a higher expectations of product."
  • Robert Ohmes, BofA: Requested details on 2026 tailwinds. Wilkes highlighted product introductions and lens simplification, stating, "We are looking at simplifying our lens assortment, especially once we have our Tier 4 lens introduced."
  • Angus Kelleher-Ferguson, Barclays: Asked about the scalability of consultative selling. Wilkes responded, "It is a multiyear journey to get our associate base to the point where we want them to be."
  • Matt Koranda, ROTH Capital: Sought clarity on the comp guide and AI glasses. Laden said, "It's really going to be a healthy mix of both our frame assortment evolution and lens leadership."
  • Lauren Ng, Morgan Stanley: Asked about comp expectations by customer cohort. Laden stated, "At the high end that we can see a positive comp across both kind of consumer cohorts."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a cautiously optimistic tone, pressing management on the durability of strategic shifts, the potential drag from cash pay customers, and the scalability of new initiatives.
  • Management’s tone was confident during prepared remarks, with Wilkes and Laden articulating clear strategies and emphasizing transformation, though some conservatism appeared in responses about future traffic and cash pay consumer trends.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone remained probing and slightly skeptical, while management continued to project confidence but with more nuance regarding the pace of change and cohort mix.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for fiscal 2026 maintains the revenue growth trajectory, but the company is shifting further into premium product offerings and targeted customer segmentation.
  • Management's tone remains optimistic but is increasingly emphasizing the multi-year nature of the transformation and the challenges in shifting customer segments.
  • Analysts continue to probe on traffic trends, pricing sustainability, and scalability of initiatives, similar to the previous quarter.
  • Key metrics such as revenue, operating income, and margin showed strong year-over-year improvement, with the 53rd week providing a one-time boost in Q4 2025.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged ongoing softness among cash pay customers and macroeconomic uncertainty impacting traffic.
  • The company is "still being a bit more conservative on our traffic expectations with the cash pay consumer until we start to see a bit more acceleration in the repurchase cycle."
  • Potential weather disruptions were noted, with Wilkes reporting, "about 15% of our fleet that sat in -- right in the crosshairs of the winter storms."
  • The scalability of consultative selling is a multi-year effort, with Wilkes acknowledging the challenge of "moving 14,000 people along the journey."

Final Takeaway

National Vision management highlighted a transformative 2025, marked by significant operating margin expansion, a deliberate shift toward more profitable customer cohorts, and substantial investments in premium products and technology. Strategic priorities for 2026 include continued revenue growth to a range of $2.03 billion to $2.09 billion, further evolution of the product and pricing mix, and disciplined cost management. The ongoing multi-year transformation focuses on enhancing the customer experience and optimizing the store fleet, while management remains cautious regarding macroeconomic challenges and the pace of change among self-pay customers.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SSR Mining double upgraded to Buy at BofA after Copler mine sale
data.description:
SSR Mining (SSRM) +15.3% in Wednesday's trading to a 15-year high of $33.37 following the sale of its 80% ownership stake in the troubled and non-operating Copler gold mine in Turkey for $1.5B.

The mine has been under care and maintenance since Turkish regulators revoked its environmental permits following a fatal accident in February 2024 caused by a landslide around the heap leach, and SSR Mining (SSRM) said it would take 24-36 months to complete repairs on the mine at a cost of $250M-$300M.

The sale prompted Bank of America analyst Lawson Winder to double-upgrade shares to Buy from Underperform and nearly doubling its NAV-based price target to $45.50 from $23, reflecting the company's substantially reduced emerging market exposure and increased developed market exposure, with North America now accounting for 93% of estimated net asset value.

Winder had removed Copler from his estimates, seeing SSR Mining (SSRM) as unlikely to obtain the required operating approvals given the landslide around the heap leach in 2024.

SSR Mining (SSRM) also said it was reviewing its position in the Hod Maden development project in Turkey, whose exit Winder said he would view positively if it happens.

data.title: Most and least shorted REIT stocks with over $2B market cap
data.description:
Retail REITs lead in short interest at the end of February, with NETSTREIT (NTST) topping the list at 28.63%, marking the highest short interest among the featured real estate stocks and signaling heightened bearish sentiment in the retail-focused REIT segment. In the top five short interest list, we have two office REITs tickers.

Short interest is the percentage of shares outstanding that have been sold short but have not yet been covered or closed out. Extremely high short interest shows investors are very pessimistic.

Seeking Alpha has compiled a list of the most and least shorted REIT stocks (with a market cap over $2B) in the current market scenario.

Most shorted stocks:

  • NETSTREIT (NTST) - 28.63% of shares outstanding
  • Medical Properties Trust (MPT) - 23.25% of shares outstanding
  • Park Hotels & Resorts (PK) - 17.37% of shares outstanding
  • Douglas Emmett (DEI) - 16.61% of shares outstanding
  • SL Green Realty (SLG) - 14.60% of shares outstanding

Least shorted stocks:

  • Prologis (PLD) - 1.21% of shares outstanding
  • The St. Joe (JOE) - 1.30% of shares outstanding
  • CBRE Group (CBRE) - 1.38% of shares outstanding
  • Sun Communities (SUI) - 1.39% of shares outstanding
  • American Tower (AMT) - 1.45% of shares outstanding

data.title: Nvidia CEO Huang says firm's $30B investment in OpenAI likely a prelude to IPO
data.description:
• Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang said on Wednesday that his company's recent $30B investment into OpenAI (OPENAI) “might be the last time” it invests in the ChatGPT maker, as it is likely to go public. 
  • Speaking at the Morgan Stanley Technology, Media & Telecom Conference, Huang said the proposed $100B investment into OpenAI, which the two companies mentioned in September, was likely “not in the cards.” 
  • “The reason for that is because they’re going to go public,” Huang said, according to comments obtained by CNBC. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman is set to appear at the conference on Thursday. 
  • Huang also noted that Nvidia's $10B investment into Anthropic (ANTHRO) would also likely be its last.
  • Nvidia shares rose 2.4% in midday trading on Wednesday.

data.title: Ross Stores' ambitious targets are achievable given standout quarter -- analysts
data.description:
Driven by higher comparable store sales, a refreshed merchandise selection, and limited price hikes despite tariff pressures—especially in home furnishings—Ross Stores (ROST) delivered an impressive quarter, beating on both the top- and bottom-line and setting guidance that appears conservative despite forecasts for 7%-8% Q1 comparable store sales.  

Reiterated as one of his top picks, Jefferies’ Corey Tarlowe called it “a standout Q4,” with broad-based strength, improving merchandising margins, and disciplined inventory.

Forecasts for 7% to 8% comparable sales growth in the current quarter surprised most analysts given Ross Stores’ tendency to set conservative guidance. But the lofty goal is surprisingly achievable thanks to improvements to marketing and the in-store experience, as well as improvements in the merchandise selection.

Additionally, while many other retailers are bracing for tighter consumer spending—especially in an environment of higher fuel prices—Ross Stores (ROST) “has a long history of being able to grow in good and bad macroeconomic environments,” BofA Securities’ Lorraine Hutchinson said in her note to clients.

To achieve the ambitious sales goals the company set for itself, there are several levers at its disposal that will generate increased traffic and drive double-digit revenue growth. Evercore ISI’s Michael Binetti points to Ross Stores’ (ROST) ongoing marketing strategy, improving in-store efficiency (with self-checkout), more inventory, and more productive new stores, all of which will enable the company to meet or exceed guidance.

While the future looks bright, investors should be mindful that Ross Stores (ROST) has been investing heavily in new distribution centers with high operational costs that could pressure margins. 

Additionally, as Seeking Alpha analyst Dividend Yield Theorist also points out, Ross (ROST) buys inventory “opportunistically,” which means it is vulnerable to inconsistencies “when costs become inventory and when revenue hits the financials, creating a possible lumpiness effect on earnings.”

However, Ross Stores (ROST) has so far proven adept at managing these headwinds—including tariffs—more effectively than peers. Its 53% year-over-year share price gain, compared with 31% for TJX Companies (TJX) and 16% for the S&P 500, suggests investors remain confident in its ability to weather further challenges.

“With improving merchandise margins, easing tariff pressure, and accelerating store growth, we believe ROST remains well positioned to gain share and drive steady EBIT expansion over time,” Tarlowe adds.

Buoyed by Tuesday's results, guidance, and a dividend hike, shares of Ross Stores (ROST) set another all-time high on Wednesday.

data.title: Hyperscalers have reached ‘peak negativity’ about capex – analyst
data.description:
The major hyperscalers have reached “peak negativity” when it comes to investor concerns about their massive spending, according to Barton Crockett, senior analyst at Rosenblatt Securities.

In an interview with CNBC, Crockett argued that while capital expenditure at Amazon (AMZN), Google/Alphabet (GOOGL), (GOOG), Meta (META), and Microsoft (MSFT) has surged dramatically, the narrative is poised to improve as spending levels stabilize.

The numbers behind the concern are stark: aggregate capex for the four major hyperscalers has grown approximately 66% year-over-year, a surge that Crockett says is “basically evaporating free cash flow at those companies.”

However, he believes the worst of the anxiety may be behind investors. “I think you’re at peak concern about the spending, peak concern about their ability to convert that into profits,” Crockett said.

In contrast to the hyperscalers, Apple (AAPL) stands out for its relative stability. The company is “not plowing capex into huge data centers like the hyperscalers,” allowing it to navigate current market turbulence more effectively.

Crockett described Apple’s (AAPL) ability to remain steady despite memory cost surges and supply chain issues as “a flex” that speaks to the company’s power, adding that recent announcements are consistent with meaningful gross margin improvement in the coming months.

Among the hyperscalers, Crockett identified Meta (META) as a top recommendation due to strong revenue growth and expected returns on investment.

He also pointed to Amazon (AMZN) as an interesting opportunity if the company can manage “the noise with Anthropic (ANTHRO).”

The analyst suggested that the current hardware-focused cycle could give way to gains in services and software stocks.

On Netflix (NFLX), Crockett maintains a hold rating, calling it “a well-managed company in a maturing environment.” He noted that while the company’s EBITDA is growing at about 20%, challenges loom as younger viewers shift toward user-generated content platforms where Netflix is less competitive. Viewership growth, he said, is no longer “rocket ship” territory.

Looking ahead, Crockett predicts a shift in the tech narrative as hyperscalers begin to “milk the revenue from the investments that they’ve made.”

He expressed confidence that capex growth will not repeat the two-thirds surge next year, creating a potentially more favorable setup for these companies as they transition from heavy spending to revenue realization.
